# Discoverable Registries

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

> **Vocabulary update (per JOS-D0077, 2026-05-20):** the canonical
> phrase is now **"Component Registry"** (singular, one per repo) — the
> single source-of-truth list of every component. Sub-views (per
> substrate-type, per domain, per lens) are **indexes**, not registries.
> See [JOS-SPEC-021] for strict Registry / Index / Manifest distinction
> + no-mix rules.

## The Standard

Every repo SHOULD have a **Component Registry** (one, canonical) at
`.jos/registry.yaml` listing every component. Sub-views (per substrate-
type, per domain, per lens) follow a three-part pattern:

1. **Registry**: ONE per repo. The authoritative list of all components
   with metadata (name, description, status, ownership, capabilities).
   Auto-derived from substrate-file frontmatter; never hand-edited.
2. **Scoping Rules**: Who can see which entries. Scope follows
   organizational boundaries.
3. **Surfacing Mechanism**: How consumers find and browse the registry
   (browsable UI, queryable API, searchable index, the rendered
   cheatsheet at `.jos/COMPONENTS-CHEATSHEET.md` once Phase 3 lands).

## What Good Looks Like

- A new participant can find the registry within 30 seconds.
- The Component Registry is the authoritative list — if it's not in the
  registry, the component doesn't exist (per JOS-P07).
- Registry entries include enough metadata for triage (should I look deeper?).
- The registry is machine-readable AND human-readable.
- Sub-views (per-substrate `_index.yaml`, per-domain `INDEX-<DOMAIN>.md`,
  the cheatsheet) are auto-rendered FROM the registry; never hand-edited
  (per JOS-SPEC-021 strict no-mix rules).

## Gap Between Standard and Reality = The Roadmap

If a repo has no Component Registry, that's a gap. If the registry
exists but isn't scoped, that's a gap. If the registry exists but isn't
surfaced to consumers (via cheatsheet + per-substrate indexes), that's
a gap.

## Connected Objects

- JOS-C14 (Ambient Discoverability) — registries are the mechanism for ambient discoverability
- **JOS-C49** (Components as Parts on a Shelf) — extends C14 with the parts-on-a-shelf framing + `component` vocabulary
- JOS-P07 (Ambient Discoverability) — the principle this standard implements
- JOS-R02 (Registry Entry Required) — the testable enforcement of this standard
- **JOS-D0077** — Components-as-Parts-on-a-Shelf decision (parent ratification)
- **JOS-SPEC-021** — Registry / Index / Manifest schema (no-mix rules)

### Framework Connection

Framework §2 (lens-based discovery), §8 (resolver for registry lookup), and §13 (four-layer identity model) together define how registries become discoverable — lenses provide navigation, resolvers provide lookup, and identity layers provide stable addressing.
